exercises.exerciseTypeName: A: Multiple Choice (The Age of Exploration)
exercises.title: A: Multiple Choice (The Age of Exploration)
exercises.instructions: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C, or D).
exercises.studentInstruction: Choose the correct answer (A, B, C, or D).
exercises.passage:
The age of exploration (1) .......... a lasting difference to our understanding of the world, and many historians see it as the beginning of the modern (2) .......... age. Ancient sea charts and maps were (3) .......... in libraries across Europe, showing that early sailors knew more about distant lands than many had believed. The (4) .......... around the Mediterranean Sea played a key role in the earliest voyages of discovery. The (5) .......... Greeks and Phoenicians had already crossed vast stretches of ocean long before the explorers of the fifteenth century set sail. It is (6) .......... that Christopher Columbus never accepted that he had reached a new continent. The population of the Americas at the time of European arrival was (7) .......... at around sixty million people. The (8) .......... world map — one that included all the major continents — was not produced until the early sixteenth century. Interest in exploration (9) .......... to countries across Europe as news of new discoveries reached royal courts and trading companies. Major changes took (10) .......... in trade, science, and politics as a direct result of these voyages. As time (11) .......... , improvements in navigation made longer journeys increasingly possible. Many of the explorers who made the most significant discoveries remained (12) .......... to ordinary people for hundreds of years.

If we (13) .......... the early history of exploration more carefully, we find many remarkable stories of ambition and courage. Sailors used the stars in (14) .......... to calculate their exact position at sea. Early mapmakers placed dotted lines in (15) .......... of firm borders to indicate territories that were still uncertain.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is collocation, so the correct answer is "made".

wrongOptions.A: "did" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.
wrongOptions.did: "did" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.
wrongOptions.B: "had" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.

wrongOptions.D: "took" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.
wrongOptions.took: "took" does not form the natural collocation needed here — "made" fits better.

explanationContent.usefulTip: Read the words before and after the gap together — collocations are heard as a single chunk.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Some word pairs are fixed collocations — the correct option is the one native speakers combine naturally.

explanations: Collocation → exploration **made** a lasting difference.
Modern **media** age (not journalism/programme).
Charts were **discovered** in libraries (found, not invented).
Mediterranean **area** around the sea.
**Ancient** Greeks and Phoenicians (very old civilisations).
It is **true** that Columbus never accepted…
Population was **estimated** at sixty million.
First **modern** world map with all continents.
Interest **spread** to countries across Europe.
Changes took **place** (fixed phrase).
As time **passed** , navigation improved.
Remained **unknown** to ordinary people for centuries.
Investigate → **look into** the early history.
In **order** to calculate position (fixed phrase).
In **place** of firm borders (uncertain territories).

exercises.title: C: Open Cloze (Getting into Photography)
exercises.instructions: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Write one word in each gap.
exercises.passage:
Many people look at (1) .......... stunning photographs shared online and become inspired by the art of capturing the world around them. (2) .......... that sounds like you, then perhaps it's time you tried your own camera. Photography (3) .......... be a wonderful hobby and we've written this simple guide for those of you thinking of (4) .......... it up.

**What if I don't have much money?**

Start with your smartphone. You can still take beautiful shots, as (5) .......... as you don't expect to produce so (6) .......... technical detail. Explore parks and city streets — and who knows? You might (7) .......... discover a real talent for it! Remember, though, that you (8) .......... always ask permission before photographing people you don't know.

**What kind of camera do I need?**

Depending on what you want to capture, you need to choose (9) .......... a compact and a mirrorless camera. A compact camera with automatic settings is (10) .......... great way of quickly (11) .......... your eye for composition. As well as more interesting subjects (12) .......... you might imagine, there are also creative techniques to explore! You should have no difficulty (13) .......... a style that suits you. If you want to take photography seriously, you (14) .......... need a camera with full manual controls. These cameras are much (15) .......... at giving you creative freedom and allow you to capture truly extraordinary images!

Whatever you decide, welcome to the wonderful world of photography!
explanations: Specific photographs → look at **the** stunning photographs.
Condition → **If** that sounds like you…
Ability → Photography **can** be a wonderful hobby.
Take up a hobby → thinking of **taking** it up.
As long as → **as long as** you don't expect too much.
So much + uncountable → so **much** technical detail.
Emphasis → might **even** discover a real talent.
Advice → you **should** always ask permission.
Choose between two → choose **between** a compact and a mirrorless camera.
Countable singular → **a** great way of finding your eye.
Gerund after preposition → way of **finding** your eye for composition.
Comparison → more interesting subjects **than** you might imagine.
Difficulty + gerund → no difficulty **in** finding a style.
Future necessity → you **will** need manual controls.
Comparative → much **better** at giving creative freedom.

items.sentence: The main investors have decided to pull **F** out of the construction project after costs rose sharply.
items.explanation: Withdraw from project → pull **out** of the construction project.

items.sentence: The new employee finds it difficult to get **H** on with the rest of the team.
items.explanation: Have a good relationship → get **on** with the rest of the team.

items.sentence: After reviewing all the applications, we've managed to narrow it **A** down to four strong candidates.
items.explanation: Reduce options → narrow it **down** to five candidates.

items.sentence: The committee agreed to look **B** into the noise complaints raised by local residents.
items.explanation: Investigate → look **into** the noise complaints.

items.sentence: It appears that Sophie has fallen **D** out with her sister over a minor disagreement.
items.explanation: Quarrel → fallen **out** with her sister.

items.sentence: It was Elena who came **C** up with the idea for the charity fundraising event.
items.explanation: Think of an idea → came **up** with the idea.

items.sentence: The exhibition started quietly but turned **G** out to be one of the most visited of the year.
items.explanation: Eventually become → turned **out** to be one of the most visited.

exercises.title: F: Key Word Transformation
exercises.instructions: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning. Write between two and five words.
exercises.studentInstruction: Complete the second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning. Write between two and five words.

items.sentence:
I lost my wallet once before this week. **second**
This is the ...... my wallet this week.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is ordinal experience, so the correct answer is "second time I have lost".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "second time I have lost", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
I took up playing squash six years ago. **for**
I ...... six years.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is duration, so the correct answer is "have been playing squash for".

wrongOptions.been playing squash for: This form misses the auxiliary — present perfect needs have/has + past participle.

explanationContent.commonMistake: Present perfect does not combine with finished past-time words like yesterday or last week.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Past simple is used for finished actions at a specific past time.

items.sentence:
I can't wait until I'm old enough to travel abroad alone. **forward**
I'm really ...... old enough to travel abroad alone.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is eager anticipation, so the correct answer is "looking forward to being".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "looking forward to being", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
Speeding on wet roads often causes serious accidents. **results**
Speeding on wet roads ...... serious accidents.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is cause and effect, so the correct answer is "often results in".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "often results in", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
It's pointless denying that you were there because three people saw you. **point**
There ...... you were there because three people saw you.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is pointless, so the correct answer is "is no point in denying".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "is no point in denying", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
Don't you wish you could travel around the world? **able**
Wouldn't you love ...... around the world?

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is wish about ability, so the correct answer is "to be able to travel".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "to be able to travel", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Articles show whether a noun is specific (the), new and countable (a/an), or general.

items.sentence:
I phoned the hotel to confirm that my booking was correct. **make**
I phoned the hotel to ...... my booking was correct.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is confirm, so the correct answer is "make sure that".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "make sure that", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Relative clauses add detail about a noun — choose the pronoun that matches the antecedent.

items.sentence:
I'm afraid you're too young to enter this competition. **old**
I'm afraid you ...... to enter this competition.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is insufficient age, so the correct answer is "are not old enough".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Check the word order and particles — English often needs the full chunk "are not old enough", not just one word.
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Prepositions often form fixed patterns — learn them as chunks, not word by word.

items.sentence:
My father says the city centre wasn't so crowded in the past. **used**
My father says the city centre ...... so crowded.

explanationContent.whyCorrect: The clue is past habit, so the correct answer is "never used to be".
explanationContent.commonMistake: Past simple is wrong when the sentence links past to now (for, since, already, yet, ever).
explanationContent.usefulTip: The words before the arrow in the original note are your clue — match that meaning to the gap.
explanationContent.grammarFocus: Present perfect links a past action to the present or unfinished time.

exercises.title: G: Error Correction (Extra word)
exercises.instructions: If a line is correct, press OK. If there is an extra word, write it.
exercises.studentInstruction: If the line is correct, tap OK. If there is an extra word, tap it.

items.sentence: I had a brilliant week at school! We were been working on a short film
items.explanation: Wrong auxiliary → We **were** working (remove **been**).

items.sentence: project when our teacher told to us that our work might be shown at the
items.explanation: Tell + object → told **us** (remove **to**).

items.sentence: local community centre. I was really keen on
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: getting involved because I've always had been fascinated by a
items.explanation: Present perfect → I've always **been** fascinated (remove **had**).

items.sentence: career in the cinema. She asked us to plan our scenes in
items.explanation: Career field → a career in cinema (remove **the**).

items.sentence: detail and to write a proper script. Most people found it out difficult
items.explanation: Find + adjective → found it difficult (remove **out**).

items.sentence: to develop original characters, but I didn't. I thought it would be great to
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: interview local people who were used to attend our school,
items.explanation: Used to + base verb → used to **attend** (remove **were**).

items.sentence: talking about how the school had shaped [them] their futures and given them the
items.explanation: Reflexive not needed → shaped **their** futures (remove **them**).

items.sentence: confidence to follow their ambitions. My teacher loved the idea and said I should
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: to contact some former students. I asked the head teacher for
items.explanation: Asked me to contact → asked me **contact** (remove **to**).

items.sentence: a few contact details and she provided several names. When I reached out and
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: described them what I was hoping to do, they were all delighted to
items.explanation: Describe + clause → described **what** I was hoping (remove **them**).

items.sentence: take part. I plan to film them at a local café using a small camera and record
items.explanation: This line is correct — tap **OK** (no extra word).

items.sentence: precisely that what they tell me. Film-making is quickly becoming my favourite school project!
items.explanation: Noun clause → precisely **what** they tell me (remove **that**).

exercises.title: H: Word Formation
exercises.instructions: Use the word in capitals to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.studentInstruction: Use the word in capitals to form a word that fits in the gap.
exercises.passage: Some (1) .......... are remembered for their daring, while others make decisions so poor that it seems completely (2) .......... . Consider the case of one (3) .......... who broke into a large department store late at night. After searching the aisles for valuable items, he came across an extremely (4) .......... sofa in the furniture section and decided to take a short rest. It may seem (5) .......... , but he was still fast asleep when the first security guard arrived the next morning. The guard (6) .......... called the police and reported the bizarre situation. Another (7) .......... case involved a man who stole a professional (8) .......... camera system from a local office. He managed to carry all the (9) .......... away without being noticed, but had accidentally left his identity card on the reception desk. It was used as (10) .......... against him in court, and he was found guilty within the hour.
explanations: Plural noun → Some **criminals** are remembered for their daring.
Adjective → decisions so **ridiculous** .
Person who steals → one **robber** who broke in.
Adjective → extremely **comfortable** sofa.
Adjective → It may seem **unbelievable** .
Adverb → The guard **nervously** called the police.
Adjective → Another **humorous** case.
Compound noun → professional **security** camera system.
Uncountable plural → carried all the **equipment** away.
Legal term → used as **evidence** against him.
